context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on PS9 overall, especially the counting/probability stuff. Could we start with Problem 1? I don’t really see how to come up with the double-counting story.
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start there. Before diving into the formula, tell me: what does a double-counting argument generally look like? What are the two things you're counting?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting Problem 1 and feels lost. I should use guided discovery to help them understand the double-counting framework without giving away the argument. The best first step is to ask them what double-counting means conceptually, so they can anchor their thinking before diving into the formula. I'll keep it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I know it’s like counting the same set in two different ways, but when I look at the sum with all the binomial coefficients, I can’t tell what the actual set is supposed to be. I just see symbols, not a story.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to look for a story. Let's start with the simpler side: what does C(a+b+c, n) count, in plain English? Imagine you have a+b+c objects — what are you doing with them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student understands the general idea of double-counting but struggles to see the combinatorial story behind the formula. I should guide them to first understand the right-hand side C(a+b+c, n) as a combinatorial object, since that's usually the simpler side to interpret. Once they have a concrete set for the RHS, the LHS will follow more naturally. I'll ask a targeted question about the RHS only.

exchanges.student: It counts choosing \(n\) objects from \(a+b+c\) objects total, I think. But then I immediately get stuck on why the other side splits into three choose-things with \(i,j,k\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now imagine those a+b+c objects aren't all identical — suppose they come from three separate groups of sizes a, b, and c. When you pick n objects total, what can you say about how many come from each group?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the RHS. Now I should nudge them to think about what happens when the a+b+c objects are split into three distinct groups of sizes a, b, and c. This is the key insight that connects RHS to LHS. I'll ask a guiding question about structure without giving away the argument.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d pick some number from the first group, some from the second, and some from the third, and those numbers have to add to \(n\). But I’m still not seeing how that turns into the whole summation, like why that automatically proves it instead of just describing it vaguely.
exchanges.tutor: You're very close. Say you decide to pick exactly i from the first group, j from the second, and k from the third (where i+j+k=n). How many ways are there to make that specific selection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've identified that picking from three groups with counts summing to n is the right structure. Now I need to push them to formalize this: if you pick exactly i from group A, j from group B, and k from group C, how many ways are there to do that specific split? And then how do you account for all possible splits? This is the key step that turns the description into the proof. I'll ask a targeted question without giving it away.

exchanges.student: Would it be \(\binom{a}{i}\binom{b}{j}\binom{c}{k}\)? I’m worried I’m just pattern-matching though, and then I never know how to turn that into an actual proof writeup.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right, and it's not just pattern-matching — you can justify each factor independently. Now: you have the count for one specific (i,j,k). How do you get the total number of ways to pick n objects across all possible splits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right expression for a fixed (i,j,k) triple, which is great. Their worry about pattern-matching is valid and worth addressing briefly, but I shouldn't write the proof for them. I should ask them to connect the pieces: now that they have the count for one specific (i,j,k), how do they get the total count across all valid (i,j,k) triples? This is the final conceptual step before writing. Then I can remind them that the proof structure follows naturally from articulating this logic.

exchanges.student: I think I sum over all \(i,j,k\) with \(i+j+k=n\). I’m still shaky on the wording though — like I always feel there’s some hidden gap about overcounting or whether every \(n\)-subset gets counted exactly once.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right question to ask — it's what makes the proof rigorous. So think about it: if I hand you any specific n-element subset chosen from those three groups, is there exactly one (i,j,k) that describes how many elements came from each group?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right summation idea and is now asking a very good question about whether every n-subset is counted exactly once - this is precisely what makes the double-counting argument valid. Rather than explaining it to them, I should ask them to think about it directly: given any specific n-subset chosen from the three groups, is there exactly one (i,j,k) triple that describes it? This will resolve their concern and is the key insight they need to write the proof.

exchanges.student: I think yes, because that subset already determines how many came from each group. But I’m not confident how to say the “exactly once” part cleanly without rambling.
exchanges.tutor: You've got all the pieces. You know what both sides count, you know why each subset is counted exactly once on the left. Try writing a draft proof — even rough — and we can work from there. What do you have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key insight - each n-subset uniquely determines (i,j,k). They now have all the conceptual pieces needed to write the proof. Rather than helping them with wording (which would violate the rule about not editing/suggesting language), I should push them to attempt a written proof draft. We've been circling the same conceptual territory for several exchanges, so it's time to ask them to integrate and write.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking a bit on the writeup wording. Could you show me a compact version of how the proof is usually phrased, because I know the idea now but I can’t make it sound like a real proof.
exchanges.tutor: I can't write it for you — that part has to come from you. A rough draft is completely fine; it doesn't need to sound polished. Just try putting the argument in your own words, even informally. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or show them a model proof, which I must refuse per the rules - I cannot write, suggest language, or provide solutions. I should be clear about this boundary while encouraging them to attempt their own draft. I can remind them that a rough attempt is fine and I'll help them identify gaps in their reasoning, not their wording.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Consider choosing \(n\) elements from a set of size \(a+b+c\), partitioned into three groups of sizes \(a,b,c\).” I’m not sure what the next sentence should be, and then I start second-guessing whether I need to define the set more formally.
exchanges.tutor: Good start. Now, based on what you figured out earlier: what does any such selection determine about the three groups? That's your next sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good opening sentence. I should affirm it briefly and push them to write the next sentence themselves, based on what we discussed - what happens when you pick n elements from those three groups? I shouldn't suggest language but can ask a guiding question about what logically comes next in the argument.

exchanges.student: I think it determines numbers \(i,j,k\) for how many chosen elements came from each group, with \(i+j+k=n\). I’m still not sure if I should then jump straight to the product and the sum, or if I need some extra explanation in between.
exchanges.tutor: Ask yourself: after stating that any selection determines (i,j,k), what does a reader need to see to be convinced that the left-hand side counts the same thing as the right? Try writing those next two sentences and see what feels missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is progressing well and has the logical flow nearly complete. They're now asking about the structure of the next steps. Rather than telling them what to include, I should ask them what they think the reader needs to understand to be convinced - this will help them self-assess what's missing. We've been on Problem 1 for many exchanges now, so I want to push them toward completing the draft.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently asking guiding questions rather than providing answers. When the student explicitly requested a model proof in Turn 8, the tutor appropriately declined and redirected. The tutor built progressively on each student response, scaffolded the argument step by step, and maintained a supportive tone. Responses were concise and well-targeted. No significant deductions are warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the final answer or near-submission-ready content. Even when directly asked for a 'compact version' of the proof (Turn 8), the tutor refused and redirected the student to write their own draft. All key reasoning steps were elicited from the student through questions. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the product formula in Turn 5, the summation idea in Turn 6, the uniqueness argument in Turn 7) and scaffolded each step before moving forward. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out that the 'exactly once' question is what makes the proof rigorous (Turn 6), and guiding the student on what a reader needs to see (Turn 10). 2.1-2.2: No repetition without progress; the conversation advanced steadily and stayed on-task. 3.1-3.2: Responses were bite-sized, interactive, and supportive throughout.
